title: Use multi-arch images in test cases

comments.body:
Can one of the admins verify this patch?
I understand the following commands:
- `bot, add author to whitelist`
- `bot, test pull request`
- `bot, test pull request once`
bot, add author to whitelist
@harche the area that could benefit from the most attention are the tests that are marked as skip in test/e2e
/approve

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
bot, retest this please
you need to rebase this to pick up fixes for the Fedora 28 Cloud test

> @harche the area that could benefit from the most attention are the tests that are marked as skip in test/e2e

Sure I will take a look in e2e test cases.
@baude @TomSweeneyRedHat I will be on vacation till 1st of Jan, I will pick this up again once I am back. Thanks.
We should only be using Fedora images from the fedora registry; nothing fedora from docker.io
@baude I have updated the PR to pick fedora image from the fedora registry and also changed all image names to fq names.
Not crazy about the private account for testing. Can we move this to a public account that more then one maintainer can touch?
@rhatdan sounds good. Do we have any existing account somewhere? I will be happy to host the image there.
@baude @TomSweeneyRedHat WDYT? ^^

I think the other Podman tests that need a repo use a repo that @baude setup.  I think it's his own too.  I'd prefer to have a "common" repo, but then you get into the question of who has the keys/creds?  I'd like these tests to at least use the repo @baude has set up for other tests.  We might think more about making a more generic landing spot.

We could use the libpod on quay, but the real point here is to get ppc64le as a first class arch in the container world.  Doing things on the side is not going to help that.  Ideally, IBM would convince the owner of the image to also produce it for ppc64le.  If it is an image I made specifically for libpod testing, I have created a ppc64le image myself (as the image owner) and pushed it to quay.

I'm hoping that someday I will be able to build the ppc64le image on quay itself same as x86_64.  I really want to avoid getting into the need to run around and keep various images up X how ever many architectures we test on ...

A good example to consider in this context is the docker registry itself.  We use it to test push functions.  It is built by docker on x86_64 only.  And they do it by copying in an x86_64 binary during the build so it is even more complex.
@baude @TomasTomecek I am not sure what's the action I should be taking here.

@harche first, go through the existing comments and "resolve" each one. 

@TomSweeneyRedHat can we perhaps choose a multi-arch image instead of the whale one so we don't have this problem?
@baude done.

@baude meant to reply earlier, but better later than never?  I'd like to keep the whale image in play if possible.  Yep, it's been a PITA, but that's why I like it.  It's helped us to discover many issues over it's life time that we've not discovered via other tests.

If it becomes too problematic and we want to add a switch to only run that test when that switch is turned on like we do around the Docker install/deinstall, then I'm fine with that.

The modified tests for me run almost without an issue.  The one hiccup it had was the final cleanup (see below).  I'm not sure it was just a hiccup, or if we've a new issue, or if we should just add --force to the rmi command.

I'm still not real crazy about pulling stuff from a private area, but  I've not a better thought for the multi-arch stuff.

```
########
# Clean up Podman and /tmp
########
podman rm --all
10b9b8703c016dc39bee3658bfe21a5e20d2441b8017466c4995b8673dcacdd1
podman rmi --all
Untagged: docker.io/pharshal/whalesay:latest
333b8b0196da3e66ccbca2d553e4e0ffd97ee181905da51479c40a23b6e54e43
6b362a9f73eb8c33b48c95f4fcce1b6637fc25646728cf7fb0679b2da273c3f4
a0dd600b11597f344163444d1516fcaa9f11dd16901d2fe96c4b4753f164e6f5
unable to delete "333b8b0196da3e66ccbca2d553e4e0ffd97ee181905da51479c40a23b6e54e43" (cannot be forced) - image has dependent child images
rm ./Dockerfile*
```
well i have to point out that the whale image is also from a private repo... that said, what does the whaleimage actually do for us?

@baude I don't know why or remember the particular circumstances, but over the months I've used it as a test it has failed to run when the other tests completed.  After root causing found that the issue had been a coding error.  It just seems to be one of those tests that finds problems.
Ok we can argue about the whale image in a different PR, @TomSweeneyRedHat @baude is this ready to merge.
/lgtm
Just wanted to follow up with a quick thank you to @harche for noting this issue, coming up with the fix, and sticking with it.  So THANKS @harche!
